43	\section{Setup and motivation}
44	\label{sec:codim1_setup}
45	
46	Fix an algebraically closed field \(\bar k\) throughout. By a \emph{variety} over \(\bar k\)
47	we mean a geometrically integral, separated scheme of finite type over \(\bar k\)
48	(Milne's convention, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, p.~v); a variety is \emph{nonsingular}
49	(or \emph{smooth}) if its local ring at every closed point is regular. A variety is
50	\emph{complete} if it is proper over \(\bar k\).
51	
52	A \emph{rational map} \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y\) between varieties is the equivalence
53	class of a regular morphism \(\varphi_U \colon U \to Y\) defined on a dense open subset
54	\(U \subseteq X\), where two pairs \((U, \varphi_U)\), \((U', \varphi_{U'})\) are equivalent
55	if \(\varphi_U\) and \(\varphi_{U'}\) agree on \(U \cap U'\) (Milne~\S I.3, p.~16). The map \(f\)
56	is \emph{defined at} a point \(x \in X\) if there is some representative \((U, \varphi_U)\)
57	with \(x \in U\); the set of such \(x\) is an open subset \(\mathrm{Dom}(f) \subseteq X\),
58	the \emph{domain of definition} of \(f\).
59	
60	The motivating question of this chapter, following Milne~\S I.3, is:
61	\begin{quote}
62	  \emph{When does a rational map \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y\) extend to a regular
63	  morphism on all of \(X\)?}
64	\end{quote}
65	Without hypotheses on \(X\) or \(Y\) the question has no positive answer (Milne lists three
66	counterexamples on p.~16: the inclusion of a proper open into the ambient variety; the
67	rational map to the cuspidal cubic whose inverse fails to extend; the inverse of a
68	blow-up at a smooth point). The two structural inputs that buy unconditional extension
69	in our setting are:
70	\begin{itemize}
71	  \item \emph{Target completeness:} if \(Y\) is complete (proper over \(\bar k\)), the
72	    valuative criterion of properness forces the rational map to extend across every
73	    codim-\(1\) point of a normal source \(X\) (Milne Theorem~3.1).
74	  \item \emph{Target group structure:} if \(Y\) is a group variety, the difference map
75	    \(\Phi(x, y) := f(x) \cdot f(y)^{-1}\) on \(X \times X\) has a vanishing/pole divisor
76	    along the diagonal that controls the codimension of the indeterminacy locus
77	    (Milne Lemma~3.3).
78	\end{itemize}
79	For \(Y\) an abelian variety both conditions hold, and combining them yields Milne's
80	Theorem~3.2 (the headline of \cref{chap:Albanese_Thm32RationalMapExtension}). The
81	present chapter packages the two inputs separately:
82	\cref{thm:codim_one_extension} is Milne~3.1, and
83	\cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} is Milne~3.3. The Weil-divisor reformulation
84	\cref{thm:weil_divisor_obstruction} translates the codim-\(1\) extension obstruction
85	in Milne~3.1's proof into the order-at-a-prime-divisor language pinned in
86	\cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}, which is the form the Lean prover consumes when
87	working with the project's \(\mathrm{Scheme.WeilDivisor}\) API.
88	
89	\section{The indeterminacy locus of a rational map}
90	\label{sec:indeterm_locus}
91	
92	\begin{definition}
93	
94	\leanok
95	  [Indeterminacy locus of a rational map]
96	  \label{def:indeterminacy_locus}
97	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.indeterminacyLocus}
98	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], \S I.3, p.~16, the paragraph "Let \(V\) and \(W\)
99	  % be varieties over \(k\)\ldots" defining the domain of definition of a rational map
100	  % (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 22).
101	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "A rational map \(\varphi\) is said to be \emph{defined} at a point
102	  % \(v\) of \(V\) if \(v \in U\) for some \((U, \varphi_U) \in \varphi\). The set \(U_1\) of
103	  % \(v\) at which \(\varphi\) is defined is open, and there is a regular map
104	  % \(\varphi_1 \colon U_1 \to W\) such that \((U_1, \varphi_1) \in \varphi\) --- clearly,
105	  % \(U_1 = \bigcup_{(U, \varphi_U) \in \varphi} U\) and we can define \(\varphi_1\) to be
106	  % the regular map such that \(\varphi_1 | U = \varphi_U\) for all $(U, \varphi_U) \in
107	  % \varphi$."
108	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, \S I.3, p.~16.}
109	  Let \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y\) be a rational map between varieties over \(\bar k\).
110	  The \emph{domain of definition} of \(f\) is the open set
111	  \[
112	    \mathrm{Dom}(f) \;:=\; \bigcup_{(U, \varphi_U) \in f} U \;\subseteq\; X,
113	  \]
114	  i.e.\ the union of the dense opens of all representatives of \(f\). The
115	  \emph{indeterminacy locus} of \(f\) is the closed complement
116	  \[
117	    Z(f) \;:=\; X \smallsetminus \mathrm{Dom}(f) \;\subseteq\; X.
118	  \]
119	  The map \(f\) is regular (i.e.\ defined on all of \(X\)) if and only if \(Z(f) = \emptyset\).
120	
121	  \paragraph{Lean encoding scope.} The Lean target
122	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.indeterminacyLocus} is a method on the
123	  Mathlib \texttt{Scheme.RationalMap} structure pinned at
124	  \texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Birational.RationalMap}; it returns a
125	  \texttt{Set X} (the underlying carrier of the closed complement of
126	  \texttt{Scheme.RationalMap.domain}, which is already exposed by Mathlib). The
127	  closedness of \(Z(f)\) is a separate \texttt{IsClosed} witness, packaged either as a
128	  field of \texttt{indeterminacyLocus} or as a standalone lemma
129	  \texttt{Scheme.RationalMap.isClosed\_indeterminacyLocus}, whichever is cleaner with
130	  Mathlib's existing API at commit \texttt{b80f227}.
131	\end{definition}
132	
133	\begin{definition}
134	
135	\leanok
136	  [Codimension-1-indeterminacy-free rational maps]
137	  \label{def:codim_one_indeterminacy}
138	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.CodimOneFree}
139	  
140	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Theorem~3.1, \S I.3, p.~16 --- the conclusion
141	  % "an open subset \(U\) of \(V\) whose complement \(V \smallsetminus U\) has codimension \(\geq 2\)"
142	  % is the standing predicate we abstract (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf,
143	  % PDF page 22).
144	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "A rational map \(\varphi \colon V \dashrightarrow W\) from a normal
145	  % variety \(V\) to a complete variety \(W\) is defined on an open subset \(U\) of \(V\) whose
146	  % complement \(V \smallsetminus U\) has codimension \(\geq 2\)."
147	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Theorem~3.1, \S I.3, p.~16 (the
148	  conclusion).}
149	  Let \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y\) be a rational map between varieties over \(\bar k\).
150	  We say \(f\) is \emph{codim-1-indeterminacy-free} (or has \emph{codimension-\(\geq 2\)
151	  indeterminacy}) if every irreducible component of the closed subset \(Z(f) \subseteq X\)
152	  has codimension \(\geq 2\) in \(X\). Equivalently, no prime divisor of \(X\) is contained in
153	  \(Z(f)\); equivalently, every point \(\eta \in X\) with \(\mathrm{codim}_X \overline{\{\eta\}} = 1\)
154	  lies in \(\mathrm{Dom}(f)\).
155	
156	  \paragraph{Why the abstraction.} Milne's Theorem~3.1 (\cref{thm:codim_one_extension}
157	  below) and Lemma~3.3 (\cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} below) both produce
158	  conclusions about the codimension of \(Z(f)\). Phrasing the codim-\(\geq 2\) condition
159	  as a named predicate keeps the cited statements concise and lets the consumer
160	  \cref{chap:Albanese_Thm32RationalMapExtension} read off the combination
161	  (\(Z(f)\) has codim \(\geq 2\) from \cref{thm:codim_one_extension} \emph{and} \(Z(f)\) is
162	  empty or pure codim 1 from \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} \(\Rightarrow\) \(Z(f)\)
163	  is empty) as a single line.
164	
165	  \paragraph{Lean encoding scope.} The Lean target
166	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.CodimOneFree} is a \texttt{Prop}-valued
167	  predicate, encoded via Mathlib's \texttt{Order.coheight} on the specialisation
168	  preorder of \texttt{X.carrier} as in
169	  \cref{def:prime_divisor}: \(f\) is codim-1-indeterminacy-free if and only if for every
170	  \(\eta \in X\) with \(\texttt{Order.coheight\ }\eta = 1\), $\eta \in
171	  \texttt{f.domain}\(. The encoding matches the standing \)\mathrm{Order.coheight}$
172	  convention pinned in \cref{def:prime_divisor}, so prime divisors of \(X\) and the
173	  hypothesis ``no codim-\(1\) indeterminacy'' use the same Mathlib idiom.
174	\end{definition}

176	\section{Smoothness yields a DVR at every codim-1 point}
177	\label{sec:smooth_dvr}
178	
179	The codim-\(1\) extension argument in Milne's proof of Theorem~3.1 hinges on the local
180	ring at the generic point of a prime divisor being a discrete valuation ring (so that
181	the valuative criterion of properness applies). On a normal variety this is the
182	defining property of regularity in codimension one; on a smooth variety it is automatic.
183	
184	The smooth-implies-regular-local-stalk implication itself is the Jacobian-criterion
185	direction of Stacks tag \texttt{00TT}; we package it as a separate sub-lemma so that
186	\cref{lem:smooth_codim_one_dvr} below reads cleanly as ``regular local of dimension
187	\(1\) is a DVR'', with the regularity prerequisite supplied by the sub-lemma.
188	
189	\subsection{Stages 5a/5b: K\"ahler-differentials localisation substrate (iter-193)}
190	\label{subsec:kaehler_localisation_substrate}
191	
192	The Jacobian-criterion direction of Stacks tag \texttt{00TT}
193	(\cref{lem:smooth_to_regular_local_ring} below) factors on the Lean side
194	through a six-stage chain. Stages~1--2 (smooth \(\Rightarrow\) flat at a
195	stalk; smooth \(\Rightarrow\) admits a standard-smooth presentation
196	locally) are axiom-clean since iter-191; Stages~3--4 (the
197	scheme-to-algebra bridge for standard-smooth presentations; freeness of
198	\(\Omega_{S/R}\) for a standard-smooth algebra \(R \to S\)) are
199	axiom-clean since iter-192. The remaining Stages~5--6 reduce the
200	Stacks~00TT conclusion to a freeness plus rank computation on K\"ahler
201	differentials, transported through localisation at the prime defining
202	the stalk. Iter-193 packaged Stages~5a (transport of freeness) and 5b
203	(matching rank under localisation) as the two standalone helpers below;
204	Stage~6 (the remaining Stacks~00OE smooth-algebra dimension formula
205	plus Stacks~02JK cotangent--K\"ahler-over-a-field bridge) is the open
206	Mathlib gap, decomposed iter-198 into the three named sub-lemmas
207	\cref{lem:smooth_algebra_krull_dim_formula} (6.A),
208	\cref{lem:cotangent_kahler_over_field} (6.B), and
209	\cref{lem:stage6_regular_stalk_assembly} (6.C) in
210	\cref{subsec:stage6_subgap_decomposition} immediately following.
211	
212	\begin{lemma}
213	
214	\leanok
215	  [Stage 5a --- K\"ahler-differentials freeness transports through localisation]
216	  \label{lem:module_free_kaehler_localization}
217	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.module_free_kaehlerDifferential_localization}
218	  Let \(R\) and \(S\) be commutative rings equipped with an \(R\)-algebra
219	  structure on \(S\), and assume that the module of K\"ahler differentials
220	  \(\Omega_{S/R}\) is free as an \(S\)-module. Let \(M \subseteq S\) be a
221	  submonoid and let \(S_M\) be a localisation of \(S\) at \(M\), so that
222	  \(R \to S \to S_M\) is a tower of \(R\)-algebras. Then the module of
223	  K\"ahler differentials \(\Omega_{S_M / R}\) is free as an
224	  \(S_M\)-module.
225	\end{lemma}
226	
227	\begin{proof}
228	  The canonical comparison map
229	  \(\Omega_{S/R} \to \Omega_{S_M/R}\) induced by the localisation map
230	  \(S \to S_M\) realises \(\Omega_{S_M/R}\) as the localisation of the
231	  \(S\)-module \(\Omega_{S/R}\) at \(M\); i.e.\ it satisfies Mathlib's
232	  \texttt{IsLocalizedModule} predicate along the underlying ring
233	  localisation. This is Stacks tag \texttt{02JK}, packaged in Mathlib as
234	  \texttt{KaehlerDifferential.isLocalizedModule\_map}. Freeness of a
235	  module is preserved under localisation of modules via Mathlib's
236	  \texttt{Module.free\_of\_isLocalizedModule}; combining the two yields
237	  the conclusion.
238	\end{proof}
239	
240	\begin{lemma}
241	
242	\leanok
243	  [Stage 5b --- K\"ahler-differentials rank under localisation equals
244	  the relative dimension]
245	  \label{lem:rank_kaehler_localization_eq_relative_dim}
246	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.rank_kaehlerDifferential_localization_eq_relativeDimension}
247	  \uses{lem:module_free_kaehler_localization}
248	  With notation as in \cref{lem:module_free_kaehler_localization},
249	  assume in addition that the \(R\)-algebra \(S\) is \emph{standard
250	  smooth of relative dimension \(n\)} (Mathlib's
251	  \texttt{Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension}\,\(n\)\,\(R\)\,\(S\),
252	  the algebra-side packaging of Stacks tag \texttt{00T7}(2)) and that
253	  the localisation \(S_M\) is non-trivial as a ring. Then the rank of
254	  \(\Omega_{S_M / R}\) over \(S_M\) equals \(n\).
255	\end{lemma}
256	
257	\begin{proof}
258	  Mathlib's
259	  \texttt{Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension.\linebreak rank\_kaehlerDifferential}
260	  asserts \(\mathrm{rank}_S(\Omega_{S/R}) = n\) under the
261	  standard-smooth hypothesis. The localisation map
262	  \(\Omega_{S/R} \to \Omega_{S_M/R}\) is an
263	  \texttt{IsLocalizedModule} along \(S \to S_M\) (Stacks tag
264	  \texttt{02JK}; cf.\ the proof of
265	  \cref{lem:module_free_kaehler_localization}), and Mathlib's
266	  \texttt{Module.lift\_rank\_of\_isLocalizedModule\_of\_free} transports
267	  module rank through an \texttt{IsLocalizedModule} up to
268	  \(\mathrm{Cardinal.lift}\). Since \(n\) is a natural number both
269	  ranks equal \(n\), so
270	  \(\mathrm{rank}_{S_M}(\Omega_{S_M / R}) = n\) as required.
271	\end{proof}

273	\subsection{Stage 6 sub-decomposition: 00OE + 02JK chain (iter-198)}
274	\label{subsec:stage6_subgap_decomposition}
275	
276	Stage 6 is the residual gap inside
277	\cref{lem:smooth_to_regular_local_ring}'s six-stage chain: given Stages
278	1--5b (a standard-smooth presentation of an affine chart
279	\(R \to S\) at a point \(z\), with \(\Omega_{S/R}\) free of rank \(n\)
280	and \(\Omega_{S_\mathfrak q / R}\) free of rank \(n\) on the stalk
281	\(S_\mathfrak q = \mathcal O_{X, z}\)), one must conclude that
282	\(S_\mathfrak q\) is a regular local ring. The Stacks proof of tag
283	\texttt{00TT} ultimately reduces to two ingredients which Mathlib does
284	not yet ship at commit \texttt{b80f227}: (6.A) the smooth-algebra
285	Krull-dimension formula (Stacks tag \texttt{00OE}, in the algebra
286	chapter as Lemma~\texttt{lemma-dimension-at-a-point-finite-type-field}),
287	and (6.B) the cotangent / K\"ahler-differential bridge over a field
288	base (Stacks tag \texttt{02JK}, in the algebra chapter as
289	Lemma~\texttt{lemma-differential-seq} plus the separable-residue-field
290	refinement of Lemma~\texttt{lemma-separable-smooth}). The two
291	ingredients combine (6.C) into the regularity conclusion via
292	\(\dim S_\mathfrak q = \mathrm{rank}_{S_\mathfrak q}\Omega_{S_\mathfrak
293	q / R} = \dim_{\kappa(\mathfrak q)} \mathfrak m / \mathfrak m^2\),
294	which is the cotangent-space characterisation of regularity that
295	Mathlib's \texttt{IsRegularLocalRing.iff\_finrank\_cotangentSpace}
296	already exposes. The next three lemmas package the three sub-gaps as
297	named iter-199+ prover targets.

299	\begin{lemma}
300	  [Stage 6.A --- Smooth-algebra Krull-dimension formula (Stacks 00OE)]
301	  \label{lem:smooth_algebra_krull_dim_formula}
302	  \lean{Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension.ringKrullDim_localization_eq_relativeDimension}
303	  \uses{lem:rank_kaehler_localization_eq_relative_dim}
304	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], lemma-dimension-at-a-point-finite-type-field
305	  % (canonically known as tag 00OE; tag-to-label cross-check not in the
306	  % local `tags/tags` file, see iter-198 writer report note) (read from
307	  % references/stacks-algebra.tex, L28206--28217 in section
308	  % "Dimension theory of finitely generated $k$-algebras").
309	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $k$ be a field. Let $S$ be a finite type $k$
310	  % algebra. Let $X = \Spec(S)$. Let $\mathfrak p \subset S$ be a prime
311	  % ideal, and let $x \in X$ be the corresponding point. Then we have
312	  % $\dim_x(X) = \dim(S_{\mathfrak p}) + \text{trdeg}_k\
313	  % \kappa(\mathfrak p)$."
314	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag~00OE (\(=\) \texttt{lemma-dimension-at-a-point-finite-type-field}).}
315	  Let \(k\) be a field, let \(S\) be a finite-type \(k\)-algebra, and let
316	  \(\mathfrak q \subset S\) be a prime ideal corresponding to a point
317	  \(x \in \mathrm{Spec}(S)\). Then
318	  \[
319	    \dim_x(\mathrm{Spec}\,S)
320	    \;=\; \dim S_{\mathfrak q} + \mathrm{trdeg}_k\,\kappa(\mathfrak q).
321	  \]
322	  In particular, if \(\mathfrak q\) is a maximal ideal (so \(x\) is a
323	  closed point) and \(k\) is algebraically closed, then
324	  \(\kappa(\mathfrak q) = k\) (Nullstellensatz), so
325	  \(\mathrm{trdeg}_k\,\kappa(\mathfrak q) = 0\) and
326	  \(\dim S_{\mathfrak q} = \dim_x(\mathrm{Spec}\,S)\).
327	
328	  \paragraph{Geometric application.} Applied to the iter-193 Stage~3
329	  setup --- \(k = \bar k\), \(S = \Gamma(X.\mathrm{left}, V)\) a
330	  standard-smooth \(\bar k\)-algebra of relative dimension \(n\) (Stage
331	  3), and \(\mathfrak q\) the prime defining the point \(z \in V\) --- the
332	  formula yields \(\dim(\mathcal O_{X, z}) = \dim S_{\mathfrak q} = n -
333	  \mathrm{trdeg}_{\bar k}\,\kappa(z)\). For \(z\) a closed point this is
334	  simply \(n\); for the generic point of a codim-\(1\) subvariety
335	  (\(\mathrm{trdeg} = n - 1\)) it is \(1\).
336	\end{lemma}

355	  % to $\text{d}f \otimes 1$."
356	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Hence we get
357	  % $$\dim_{\kappa} \Omega_{R/k} \otimes_R \kappa = \dim_\kappa
358	  % \mathfrak m/\mathfrak m^2 + \text{trdeg}_k (\kappa) \geq \dim R +
359	  % \text{trdeg}_k (\kappa) = \dim_{\mathfrak q} S$$
360	  % (see Lemma \ref{lemma-dimension-at-a-point-finite-type-field} for
361	  % the last equality) with equality if and only if $R$ is regular."
362	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag~02JK (right-exact half = \texttt{lemma-differential-seq}; separable-residue refinement inside \texttt{lemma-separable-smooth}).}
363	  Let \(k\) be a field and let \(R\) be a Noetherian local
364	  \(k\)-algebra with maximal ideal \(\mathfrak m\) and residue field
365	  \(\kappa = R/\mathfrak m\). The canonical exact sequence
366	  \[
367	    \mathfrak m / \mathfrak m^2
368	    \;\longrightarrow\; \Omega_{R/k} \otimes_R \kappa
369	    \;\longrightarrow\; \Omega_{\kappa / k}
370	    \;\longrightarrow\; 0
371	  \]
372	  identifies the cotangent space \(\mathfrak m / \mathfrak m^2\) (a
373	  \(\kappa\)-vector space) with the kernel of the right map. When
374	  \(\kappa / k\) is separable (in particular when \(k\) is
375	  algebraically closed and \(\kappa = k\), so \(\Omega_{\kappa / k}
376	  = 0\)) the leftmost map is injective and the sequence is short exact
377	  --- in fact split short exact when there is a \(k\)-algebra section
378	  \(\kappa \to R\) (e.g.\ at a closed point of a \(\bar k\)-variety).
379	  In particular, over an algebraically closed field \(k = \bar k\) and
380	  at a closed point \(\mathfrak m\),
381	  \[
382	    \Omega_{R/k} \otimes_R \kappa \;\simeq\; \mathfrak m / \mathfrak m^2
383	  \]
384	  as \(\kappa\)-vector spaces; hence
385	  \(\dim_\kappa(\Omega_{R/k} \otimes_R \kappa)
386	   = \dim_\kappa \mathfrak m / \mathfrak m^2\).
387	\end{lemma}

389	\begin{lemma}
390	  [Stage 6.C --- Assembly: smooth standard-smooth stalk is regular local]
391	  \label{lem:stage6_regular_stalk_assembly}
392	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.isRegularLocalRing_stalk_of_smooth_aux}
393	  \uses{lem:rank_kaehler_localization_eq_relative_dim,
394	        lem:smooth_algebra_krull_dim_formula,
395	        lem:cotangent_kahler_over_field}
396	  Let \(\bar k\) be an algebraically closed field, let
397	  \(R \to S\) be a standard-smooth \(\bar k\)-algebra presentation of
398	  relative dimension \(n\) on an affine chart \(\Spec S \subseteq X\),
399	  and let \(z \in \Spec S\) be a point corresponding to a prime
400	  \(\mathfrak q \subset S\). Then the stalk \(S_{\mathfrak q}\) is a
401	  regular local ring of Krull dimension
402	  \(n - \mathrm{trdeg}_{\bar k}\,\kappa(\mathfrak q)\). In the
403	  closed-point case (\(\kappa(\mathfrak q) = \bar k\),
404	  \(\mathrm{trdeg} = 0\)) the dimension equals \(n\); in the
405	  codim-\(1\) generic-point case (\(\mathrm{trdeg} = n - 1\)) the
406	  dimension equals \(1\).
407	\end{lemma}
408	
409	\begin{proof}
410	  Combine the three sub-lemmas:
411	  \begin{enumerate}
412	    \item By \cref{lem:rank_kaehler_localization_eq_relative_dim} (Stage~5b)
413	      the localised K\"ahler module \(\Omega_{S_{\mathfrak q} / R}\)
414	      is free of rank \(n\) over \(S_{\mathfrak q}\).
415	    \item By \cref{lem:smooth_algebra_krull_dim_formula} (Stage 6.A,
416	      Stacks 00OE) the Krull dimension of \(S_{\mathfrak q}\) equals
417	      \(\dim_z \Spec S - \mathrm{trdeg}_{\bar k}\,\kappa(\mathfrak q)\),
418	      which for a standard-smooth presentation of relative dimension
419	      \(n\) equals \(n - \mathrm{trdeg}_{\bar k}\,\kappa(\mathfrak q)\).
420	      (For the standard-smooth presentation the dimension
421	      \(\dim_z \Spec S = n\) follows from the global-complete-intersection
422	      structure: \(n\) variables modulo \(n - \dim\) equations, see
423	      Stacks \texttt{lemma-relative-global-complete-intersection-smooth}.)
424	    \item By \cref{lem:cotangent_kahler_over_field} (Stage 6.B,
425	      Stacks 02JK) the residue-field tensor \(\Omega_{S_{\mathfrak q}/R}
426	      \otimes_{S_{\mathfrak q}} \kappa(\mathfrak q)\) is isomorphic to
427	      \(\mathfrak m / \mathfrak m^2\) as \(\kappa(\mathfrak q)\)-vector
428	      spaces. Combined with (1), \(\dim_{\kappa(\mathfrak q)} \mathfrak
429	      m / \mathfrak m^2 = n\) at the closed-point case (and in general
430	      \(n - \mathrm{trdeg}_{\bar k}\,\kappa(\mathfrak q)\) by base-change
431	      of the standard-smooth presentation to the closed-point fibre).
432	    \item Compare (2) and (3): both \(\dim S_{\mathfrak q}\) and
433	      \(\dim_{\kappa(\mathfrak q)} \mathfrak m / \mathfrak m^2\) equal
434	      \(n - \mathrm{trdeg}_{\bar k}\,\kappa(\mathfrak q)\); the
435	      regularity criterion for Noetherian local rings (the equality
436	      \(\dim_\kappa \mathfrak m / \mathfrak m^2 = \dim R\) is the
437	      defining condition for a regular local ring, Stacks
438	      \texttt{definition-regular-local} \(=\) Mathlib
439	      \texttt{IsRegularLocalRing}) then gives \(S_{\mathfrak q}\)
440	      regular. The scheme-level transfer
441	      \(S_{\mathfrak q} = \mathcal O_{X, z}\) (Stage 3
442	      \texttt{IsLocalization.AtPrime} on the affine chart) completes
443	      the conclusion.
444	  \end{enumerate}
445	\end{proof}

669	  [Smooth \(\Rightarrow\) DVR at every codim-\(1\) point]
670	  \label{lem:smooth_codim_one_dvr}
671	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.localRing_dvr_of_codim_one}
672	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, p.~130 (the definition ``regular in codimension one'',
673	  % together with the immediately preceding remark that on a nonsingular variety every
674	  % local ring is regular) (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf,
675	  % PDF page 147).
676	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "\textbf{Definition.} We say a scheme \(X\) is \emph{regular in
677	  % codimension one} (or sometimes \emph{nonsingular in codimension one}) if every
678	  % local ring \(\mathcal O_x\) of \(X\) of dimension one is regular."
679	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "The most important examples of such schemes are nonsingular
680	  % varieties over a field (I, \S5) and noetherian normal schemes. On a nonsingular
681	  % variety the local ring of every closed point is regular (I, 5.1), hence all the
682	  % local rings are regular, since they are localizations of the local rings of
683	  % closed points."
684	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "If \(Y\) is a prime divisor on \(X\), let \(\eta \in Y\) be its generic
685	  % point. Then the local ring \(\mathcal O_{\eta, X}\) of \(X\) at \(\eta\) is a discrete
686	  % valuation ring with quotient field \(K\), the function field of \(X\)."
687	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~130 (definition of regular in codimension one;
688	  the DVR consequence at a codim-1 point).}
689	  Let \(X\) be a nonsingular variety over \(\bar k\) and let \(\eta \in X\) be a point with
690	  \(\mathrm{codim}_X \overline{\{\eta\}} = 1\). Then the local ring \(\mathcal O_{X, \eta}\) is a
691	  discrete valuation ring with fraction field the function field \(K(X)\) of \(X\).
692	\end{lemma}
693	
694	\begin{proof}
695	
696	  % NOTE (iter-185 writer): the `` marker on this proof block reflects
697	  % the Krull-dim half (Tier-1 axiom-clean closure achieved iter-184 via
698	  % `Scheme.ringKrullDim_stalk_eq_coheight` from the iter-183
699	  % `Albanese/CoheightBridge.lean`, see
700	  % `\cref{chap:Albanese_CoheightBridge}`). The `IsRegularLocalRing` half is
701	  % tracked separately as the Stacks 00TT gap and is now packaged in
702	  % `\cref{lem:smooth_to_regular_local_ring}` above; that block carries no
703	  % `` because the Mathlib hooks (i)--(iv) in its proof sketch are not
704	  % all axiom-clean at commit `b80f227`. The composition (00TT regularity +
705	  % iter-183 Krull-dim bridge) inside the private Lean helper
706	  % `smooth_codim_one_maximalIdeal_isPrincipal_and_ne_bot`
707	  % (`AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/CodimOneExtension.lean:223`) keeps a single
708	  % typed `sorry` on the regularity half pending an iter-185+ prover round.
709	  By \cref{lem:smooth_to_regular_local_ring} (Stacks tag \texttt{00TT}, the
710	  Jacobian-criterion direction), the stalk \(\mathcal O_{X, x}\) is regular for
711	  every point \(x \in X\) --- in particular at every closed point (the case
712	  Hartshorne~I.5.1 states classically). Localising a regular local ring at a
713	  prime ideal yields a regular local ring; in particular, for \(\eta \in X\) with
714	  \(\mathrm{codim}_X \overline{\{\eta\}} = 1\), the local ring
715	  \(\mathcal O_{X, \eta}\) is regular of Krull dimension \(1\). A regular local ring of Krull dimension \(1\) is exactly a
716	  discrete valuation ring (Atiyah--Macdonald, Proposition 9.2; or Stacks tag
717	  \texttt{00PD}). Its fraction field is the function field \(K(X)\) of \(X\) by
718	  irreducibility of \(X\) (the unique generic point of \(X\) lies above \(\eta\), and the
719	  stalk at the generic point of an integral scheme is the function field).
720	
721	  \paragraph{Lean encoding scope.} The Lean target
722	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.localRing\_dvr\_of\_codim\_one} is the
723	  instance/lemma stating that \(\mathcal O_{X, \eta}\) is a Mathlib
724	  \texttt{IsDiscreteValuationRing} under the typeclass set
725	  \texttt{[Smooth\,(\dots)\,X.hom]\ [IsIntegral X.left]} and the hypothesis
726	  \texttt{Order.coheight\ \(\eta\) = 1}. The fraction-field identification with
727	  \texttt{X.functionField} is a downstream named instance
728	  \texttt{Scheme.fractionRing\_localRing\_eq\_functionField} (or whatever Mathlib name
729	  the prover chooses at commit \texttt{b80f227}). The regular-local-ring-of-dimension-1
730	  \(\Leftrightarrow\) DVR equivalence is shipped in Mathlib as
731	  \texttt{IsRegularLocalRing.isDiscreteValuationRing\_of\_dim\_one} (or analogous);
732	  the prover re-exports it rather than re-proving.
733	\end{proof}

735	\section{Milne Theorem 3.1: codimension-\(\geq 2\) extension}
736	\label{sec:milne_thm31}
737	
738	The first input to Milne's Theorem~3.2 is the following: a rational map from a normal
739	variety to a \emph{complete} variety automatically extends across every codim-\(1\)
740	point.
741	
742	\begin{theorem}
743	
744	\leanok
745	  [Milne Theorem~3.1: codim-\(\geq 2\) extension to a complete target]
746	  \label{thm:codim_one_extension}
747	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.extend_of_codimOneFree_of_smooth}
748	  
749	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Theorem~3.1, \S I.3, p.~16
750	  % (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 22).
751	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "\textsc{Theorem 3.1.} \emph{A rational map $\varphi \colon V
752	  % \dashrightarrow W\( from a normal variety \)V\( to a complete variety \)W$ is defined
753	  % on an open subset \(U\) of \(V\) whose complement \(V \smallsetminus U\) has codimension
754	  % \(\geq 2\).}"
755	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Theorem~3.1, \S I.3, p.~16.}

756	  Let \(X\) be a nonsingular variety over \(\bar k\), let \(Y\) be a complete variety over
757	  \(\bar k\), and let \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y\) be a rational map. Then the
758	  indeterminacy locus \(Z(f)\) (\cref{def:indeterminacy_locus}) has codimension
759	  \(\geq 2\) in \(X\); equivalently, \(f\) is codim-\(1\)-indeterminacy-free
760	  (\cref{def:codim_one_indeterminacy}).
761	
762	  Specialising further: if in addition \(f\) \emph{is} codim-\(1\)-indeterminacy-free
763	  ``for free'' (i.e.\ no prime divisor of \(X\) is contained in \(Z(f)\)), then
764	  \(\mathrm{Dom}(f) = X\) and \(f\) extends to a regular morphism $\widetilde f \colon X
765	  \to Y\(. The extension is unique because \)X\( is reduced and \)\mathrm{Dom}(f)$ is dense
766	  in \(X\).
767	\end{theorem}
768	
769	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "\textsc{Proof.} Assume first that \(V\) is a curve. Thus we are
770	% given a nonsingular curve \(V\) and a regular map \(\varphi \colon U \to W\) from an
771	% open subset of \(V\) which we want to extend to \(V\). Consider the maps
772	% [diagram: \(U \hookrightarrow V\), $U \xrightarrow{u \mapsto (u, \varphi(u))} Z
773	% \subseteq V \times W\(, \)U \xrightarrow{\varphi} W$, with projections from
774	% \(V \times W\) to \(V\) and to \(W\)]
775	% Let \(U'\) be the image of \(U\) in \(V \times W\), and let \(Z\) be its closure. The image
776	% of \(Z\) in \(V\) is closed (because \(W\) is complete), and contains \(U\) (the composite
777	% \(U \to V\) is the given inclusion), and so \(Z\) maps onto \(V\). The maps $U \to U'
778	% \to U\( are isomorphisms. Therefore, \)Z \to V$ is a surjective map from a complete
779	% curve onto a nonsingular complete curve that is an isomorphism on open subsets.
780	% Such a map must be an isomorphism (complete nonsingular curves are determined by
781	% their function fields). The restriction of the projection map \(V \times W \to W\)
782	% to \(Z\ (\simeq V)\) is the extension of \(\varphi\) to \(V\) we are seeking.
783	%   The general case can be reduced to the one-dimensional case (using schemes). Let
784	% \(U\) be the largest subset on which \(\varphi\) is defined, and suppose that $V
785	% \smallsetminus U\( has codimension 1. Then there is a prime divisor \)Z\( in \)V
786	% \smallsetminus U\(. Because \)V$ is normal, its associated local ring is a discrete
787	% valuation ring \(\mathcal O_Z\) with field of fractions \(k(V)\). The map \(\varphi\)
788	% defines a morphism of schemes \(\operatorname{Spec}(k(V)) \to W\), which the
789	% valuative criterion of properness (Hartshorne 1977, II 4.7) shows extends to a
790	% morphism \(\operatorname{Spec}(\mathcal O_Z) \to W\). This implies that \(\varphi\)
791	% has a representative defined on an open subset that meets \(Z\) in a nonempty set,
792	% which is a contradiction."
793	\begin{proof}
794	  
795	  Following Milne's two-step proof.
796	
797	  \emph{Step 1: rule out codim-\(1\) components.} Suppose for contradiction that \(Z(f)\)
798	  contains a prime divisor \(W \subseteq X\) (i.e.\ a codim-\(1\) irreducible component).
799	  Let \(\eta \in W\) be its generic point, so \(\mathrm{codim}_X \overline{\{\eta\}} = 1\). By
800	  \cref{lem:smooth_codim_one_dvr}, the local ring \(\mathcal O_{X, \eta}\) is a discrete
801	  valuation ring with fraction field the function field \(K(X)\). Any representative
802	  \((U, \varphi_U) \in f\) has \(U \cap \mathrm{Dom}(f) \neq \emptyset\) and so determines
803	  a \(\bar k\)-algebra map from the function field \(K(Y)\) of \(Y\) to \(K(X)\): explicitly,
804	  \(\varphi_U\) defines a morphism of schemes \(\operatorname{Spec}(K(X)) \to Y\) (since
805	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(K(X))\) is the generic point of \(X\)). By the valuative criterion
806	  of properness applied to the complete variety \(Y\)
807	  (\emph{cf.}\ Hartshorne~II.4.7, cited verbatim in Milne~3.1's proof), this morphism
808	  extends to a morphism \(\operatorname{Spec}(\mathcal O_{X, \eta}) \to Y\). Standard
809	  algebraic-geometry spreading then exhibits an open neighbourhood \(V\) of \(\eta\) in
810	  \(X\) on which \(f\) has a regular representative; in particular $\eta \in
811	  \mathrm{Dom}(f)\(, contradicting \)\eta \in W \subseteq Z(f)\(. Hence no codim-\)1$
812	  component of \(X\) lies in \(Z(f)\), i.e.\ \(Z(f)\) has codimension \(\geq 2\).
813	
814	  \emph{Step 2: extend across codim-\(\geq 2\) points (the "codim-1-indeterminacy-free
815	  \(\Rightarrow\) extension" half).} Suppose now that \(Z(f)\) has codimension \(\geq 2\)
816	  but is non-empty (we want to deduce \(Z(f) = \emptyset\) under the additional input
817	  that ``\(f\) is already codim-1-indeterminacy-free, by hypothesis''). Pick a closed
818	  point \(x \in Z(f)\) with \(\mathrm{codim}_X \overline{\{x\}} \geq 2\). By
819	  \cref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay} applied to the regular local ring
820	  \(\mathcal O_{X, x}\), we have
821	  \(\mathrm{depth}(\mathcal O_{X, x}) = \dim(\mathcal O_{X, x}) \geq 2\). The sheaf
822	  \(\mathcal O_X\) on the punctured neighbourhood \(V \smallsetminus \{x\}\)
823	  (any small affine neighbourhood \(V \ni x\)) has \(H^0_{\{x\}}(V, \mathcal O_X) = 0\)
824	  and \(H^1_{\{x\}}(V, \mathcal O_X) = 0\) (the depth-\(\geq 2\) characterisation of
825	  local-cohomology vanishing, Stacks tag \texttt{0AVF}; cf.\ Hartshorne~III.3.5 for
826	  the surface case). Pull back the coordinates of any affine chart of \(Y\) around the
827	  closure \(\overline{f(\mathrm{Dom}(f) \cap V)}\) along \(f\); the resulting sections of
828	  \(\mathcal O_X\) on \(V \cap \mathrm{Dom}(f)\) extend uniquely to sections on \(V\),
829	  yielding a regular representative of \(f\) on \(V\). Hence \(x \in \mathrm{Dom}(f)\),
830	  contradicting \(x \in Z(f)\). Therefore \(Z(f) = \emptyset\) and \(f\) extends to a
831	  regular morphism \(\widetilde f \colon X \to Y\).
832	
833	  Uniqueness of \(\widetilde f\) is the standard reduced-and-separated agreement
834	  principle: two morphisms \(X \to Y\) that agree on the dense open \(\mathrm{Dom}(f)\)
835	  agree on all of \(X\).
836	\end{proof}
837	
838	\begin{remark}
839	  \label{rmk:codim1_role_of_ab}
840	  \textbf{Where Auslander--Buchsbaum enters.} Step~1 of the proof uses only normality
841	  + the valuative criterion of properness (Hartshorne~II.4.7) and is characteristic-free.
842	  Step~2 uses the depth-\(\geq 2\) extension property at a codim-\(\geq 2\) regular
843	  point, which is exactly what \cref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay} of
844	  \cref{chap:Albanese_AuslanderBuchsbaum} supplies (regular local of dimension \(d\)
845	  \(\Rightarrow\) Cohen--Macaulay of depth \(d\)). For our application the source \(X\) is a
846	  smooth variety, so the regular-local-ring hypothesis is automatic; the Cohen--Macaulay
847	  consequence is what feeds the local-cohomology vanishing.
848	
849	  Concretely, when \(X\) is a smooth surface (e.g.\ \(\mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb P^1\) or
850	  \(C \times C\) for a smooth projective curve \(C\)), the codim-\(\geq 2\) closed points of
851	  \(X\) are exactly the closed points of \(X\) (because \(\dim X = 2\)), and at each such
852	  point \(\mathcal O_{X, x}\) is regular of dimension \(2\), hence Cohen--Macaulay of
853	  depth \(2\). The smooth-surface case is the one A.4.c will invoke when extending the
854	  symmetric-product rational map \(C^{(g)} \dashrightarrow A\) along the Abel--Jacobi
855	  birational equivalence to \(J = \Pic^0_{C/k}\).
856	\end{remark}
857	
858	\section{Milne Lemma 3.3: indeterminacy locus of a map to a group variety is codim 1}
859	\label{sec:milne_lem33}
860	
861	The second input to Milne's Theorem~3.2 is structural: when the target \(Y\) is a group
862	variety, the indeterminacy locus is either empty or of \emph{pure} codimension \(1\).
863	Combined with \cref{thm:codim_one_extension}'s ``codimension \(\geq 2\)'' conclusion,
864	this forces the locus to be empty.
865	
866	\begin{lemma}
867	
868	\leanok
869	  [Milne Lemma~3.3: pure-codim-\(1\) structure of the indeterminacy locus into a group
870	  variety]
871	  \label{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy}
872	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.indeterminacy_pure_codim_one_into_grpScheme}
873	  
874	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Lemma~3.3, \S I.3, p.~17
875	  % (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 23).
876	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "\textsc{Lemma 3.3.} \emph{Let \(\varphi \colon V \dashrightarrow G\)
877	  % be a rational map from a nonsingular variety to a group variety. Then either
878	  % \(\varphi\) is defined on all of \(V\) or the points where it is not defined form a
879	  % closed subset of pure codimension 1 in \(V\) (i.e., a finite union of prime
880	  % divisors).}"
881	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Lemma~3.3, \S I.3, p.~17.}
882	  Let \(X\) be a nonsingular variety over \(\bar k\), let \(G\) be a group variety over
883	  \(\bar k\) (a smooth group scheme of finite type), and let $f \colon X \dashrightarrow
884	  G\( be a rational map. Then either \)f\( is defined on all of \)X$, or the indeterminacy
885	  locus \(Z(f) \subseteq X\) is a closed subset of pure codimension \(1\) (i.e.\ a finite
886	  union of prime divisors).
887	
888	  Equivalently: it is \emph{never} the case that \(f\) has a non-empty indeterminacy
889	  locus all of whose irreducible components have codimension \(\geq 2\).
890	\end{lemma}
891	
892	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "\textsc{Proof.} Define a rational map
893	%   \(\Phi \colon V \times V \dashrightarrow G\), $\;(x, y) \mapsto \varphi(x) \cdot
894	%   \varphi(y)^{-1}$.
895	% More precisely, if \((U, \varphi_U)\) represents \(\varphi\), then \(\Phi\) is the rational
896	% map represented by
897	%   $U \times U \xrightarrow{\varphi_U \times \varphi_U} G \times G
898	%   \xrightarrow{\mathrm{id} \times \mathrm{inv}} G
899	%   \xrightarrow{m} G$.
900	% Clearly \(\Phi\) is defined at a diagonal point \((x, x)\) if \(\varphi\) is defined at
901	% \(x\), and then \(\Phi(x, x) = e\). Conversely, if \(\Phi\) is defined at \((x, x)\), then
902	% it is defined on an open neighbourhood of \((x, x)\); in particular, there will be an
903	% open subset \(U\) of \(V\) such that \(\Phi\) is defined on \(\{x\} \times U\). After
904	% possible replacing \(U\) by a smaller open subset (not necessarily containing \(x\)),
905	% \(\varphi\) will be defined on \(U\). For \(u \in U\), the formula
906	%   \(\varphi(x) = \Phi(x, u) \cdot \varphi(u)\)
907	% defines \(\varphi\) at \(x\). Thus \(\varphi\) is defined at \(x\) if and only if \(\Phi\) is
908	% defined at \((x, x)\). The rational map \(\Phi\) defines a map
909	%   \(\Phi^\ast \colon \mathcal O_{G, e} \to k(V \times V)\).
910	% Since \(\Phi\) sends \((x, x)\) to \(e\) if it is defined there, it follows that \(\Phi\)
911	% is defined at \((x, x)\) if and only if
912	%   \(\operatorname{Im}(\mathcal O_{G, e}) \subset \mathcal O_{V \times V, (x, x)}\).
913	% Now \(V \times V\) is nonsingular, and so we have a good theory of divisors (AG,
914	% Chapter 12). For a nonzero rational function \(f\) on \(V \times V\), write
915	%   \(\operatorname{div}(f) = \operatorname{div}(f)_0 - \operatorname{div}(f)_\infty\),
916	% with \(\operatorname{div}(f)_0\) and \(\operatorname{div}(f)_\infty\) effective divisors
917	% --- note that \(\operatorname{div}(f)_\infty = \operatorname{div}(f^{-1})_0\). Then
918	%   $\mathcal O_{V \times V, (x, x)} = \{f \in k(V \times V) \mid
919	%   \operatorname{div}(f)_\infty \text{ does not contain } (x, x)\} \cup \{0\}$.
920	% Suppose \(\phi\) is not defined at \(x\). Then for some $f \in \operatorname{Im}
921	% (\varphi^\ast)\(, \)(x, x) \in \operatorname{div}(f)_\infty\(, and clearly \)\Phi$ is
922	% not defined at the points \((y, y) \in \Delta \cap \operatorname{div}(f)_\infty\).
923	% This is a subset of pure codimension one in \(\Delta\) (AG 9.2), and when we identify
924	% it with a subset of \(V\), it is a subset of \(V\) of codimension one passing through
925	% \(x\) on which \(\varphi\) is not defined."
926	\begin{proof}
927	  
928	  Following Milne's proof of Lemma~3.3. The argument has four sub-steps. Throughout we
929	  fix a representative \((U, \varphi_U)\) of \(f\).
930	
931	  \emph{Sub-step 1: the difference rational map.} Define
932	  \[
933	    \Phi \colon X \times X \dashrightarrow G,
934	    \qquad
935	    \Phi(x, y) \;:=\; \varphi(x) \cdot \varphi(y)^{-1},
936	  \]
937	  via the composition
938	  \[
939	    U \times U
940	    \;\xrightarrow{\varphi_U \times \varphi_U}\; G \times G
941	    \;\xrightarrow{\mathrm{id} \times \mathrm{inv}}\; G \times G
942	    \;\xrightarrow{m}\; G,
943	  \]
944	  where \(\mathrm{inv} \colon G \to G\) is the group-variety inverse and
945	  \(m \colon G \times G \to G\) is the group law. The map \(\Phi\) is rational by
946	  composition: its domain is at least \(U \times U \subseteq X \times X\), which is dense
947	  because \(U\) is dense in \(X\).
948	
949	  \emph{Sub-step 2: \(\Phi\)-defined at \((x, x)\) \(\Leftrightarrow\) \(\varphi\)-defined at
950	  \(x\).} If \(\varphi\) is defined at \(x\), the explicit formula \(\Phi(x, x) = e\) (the
951	  identity of \(G\)) shows \(\Phi\) is defined at \((x, x)\). Conversely, if \(\Phi\) is
952	  defined at \((x, x)\), openness of \(\mathrm{Dom}(\Phi)\) in \(X \times X\) produces an
953	  open set \(W \ni (x, x)\) on which \(\Phi\) is defined; by projection (taking the slice
954	  \(\{x\} \times W' \subseteq W\) for some open \(W' \subseteq X\), possibly shrinking
955	  \(W'\) so \(\varphi\) is defined on \(W'\) as well --- \(W'\) need not contain \(x\)), the
956	  identity
957	  \[
958	    \varphi(x) \;=\; \Phi(x, u) \cdot \varphi(u),
959	    \qquad u \in W',
960	  \]
961	  exhibits a regular representative of \(\varphi\) at \(x\). Hence $x \in
962	  \mathrm{Dom}(\varphi)\( if and only if \)(x, x) \in \mathrm{Dom}(\Phi)$.
963	
964	  \emph{Sub-step 3: rephrase via pullback of local rings.} The rational map \(\Phi\)
965	  pulls \(\mathcal O_{G, e}\) back to a \(\bar k\)-subalgebra of the function field
966	  \(K(X \times X)\):
967	  \[
968	    \Phi^\ast \colon \mathcal O_{G, e} \;\longrightarrow\; K(X \times X).
969	  \]
970	  Because \(\Phi\) sends \((x, x)\) to \(e\) wherever it is defined, \(\Phi\) is defined at
971	  \((x, x)\) if and only if $\Phi^\ast(\mathcal O_{G, e}) \subseteq \mathcal O_{X
972	  \times X, (x, x)}\( (i.e.\ every local function on \)G\( near \)e$ has no pole at
973	  \((x, x)\) after pullback).
974	
975	  \emph{Sub-step 4: pole locus is pure codimension \(1\).} The variety \(X \times X\) is
976	  nonsingular (smoothness is stable under products over \(\bar k\)), so the Weil-divisor
977	  apparatus of \cref{def:codim1_cycles} and the order map of
978	  \cref{def:order_at_point} apply on \(X \times X\). For any nonzero rational function
979	  \(f \in K(X \times X)^\ast\) decompose its divisor as
980	  \[
981	    \mathrm{div}(f) \;=\; \mathrm{div}(f)_0 - \mathrm{div}(f)_\infty,
982	  \]
983	  with \(\mathrm{div}(f)_0\) and \(\mathrm{div}(f)_\infty\) effective and
984	  \(\mathrm{div}(f)_\infty = \mathrm{div}(f^{-1})_0\) (Milne's notation, matching the
985	  project's \(\div(f) = \sum \ord_Y(f) \cdot Y\) of
986	  \cref{def:principal_divisor}: \(\mathrm{div}(f)_0\) collects the prime divisors with
987	  \(\ord_Y(f) > 0\) and \(\mathrm{div}(f)_\infty\) those with \(\ord_Y(f) < 0\)). The local
988	  ring at \((x, x)\) can be characterised as
989	  \[
990	    \mathcal O_{X \times X, (x, x)}
991	    \;=\; \bigl\{\, f \in K(X \times X) \;\big|\;
992	    \mathrm{div}(f)_\infty \text{ does not contain } (x, x) \,\bigr\} \cup \{0\}.
993	  \]
994	  Suppose \(f \in K(X \times X)^\ast\) is not in \(\mathcal O_{X \times X, (x, x)}\);
995	  equivalently, \((x, x) \in \mathrm{supp}(\mathrm{div}(f)_\infty)\). The pole divisor
996	  \(\mathrm{div}(f)_\infty\) is a finite sum of prime divisors of \(X \times X\) (i.e.\ a
997	  closed subset of pure codimension \(1\) in \(X \times X\)). Its intersection with the
998	  diagonal \(\Delta_X \subseteq X \times X\) is a closed subset of pure codimension \(1\)
999	  in \(\Delta_X\) (this is Milne's reference to AG~9.2, the standard fact that a prime
1000	  divisor of \(X \times X\) either contains \(\Delta_X\) or meets it in pure codimension
1001	  \(1\); on a nonsingular variety this is an instance of Krull's principal-ideal theorem
1002	  applied to the local equation of \(\Delta_X\) inside \(X \times X\)).
1003	
1004	  \emph{Conclusion.} Suppose \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow G\) is not defined on all of
1005	  \(X\). Pick \(x \in Z(f)\). By Sub-steps~2--3, there is some $f_0 \in
1006	  \Phi^\ast(\mathcal O_{G, e})\( with \)(x, x) \in \mathrm{supp}(\mathrm{div}(f_0)_\infty)$.
1007	  By Sub-step~4 the intersection $\mathrm{supp}(\mathrm{div}(f_0)_\infty) \cap
1008	  \Delta_X\( is pure codimension \)1\( in \)\Delta_X\(, passing through \)(x, x)$.
1009	  Identifying \(\Delta_X\) with \(X\) via the diagonal isomorphism, the corresponding
1010	  closed subset of \(X\) has pure codimension \(1\) and passes through \(x\). By Sub-step~2,
1011	  every point of this codim-\(1\) subset is a point where \(\Phi\) is undefined on the
1012	  diagonal, hence where \(\varphi\) is undefined. Thus \(Z(\varphi)\) contains a pure
1013	  codim-\(1\) closed subset passing through \(x\); since \(x \in Z(\varphi)\) was arbitrary,
1014	  every irreducible component of \(Z(\varphi)\) has codimension exactly \(1\), i.e.\
1015	  \(Z(\varphi)\) is of pure codimension \(1\).
1016	\end{proof}
1017	
1018	\begin{remark}
1019	  \label{rmk:milne_lem33_charfree}
1020	  \textbf{Characteristic-free.} The above proof uses only: (i) the group law on \(G\)
1021	  and its inverse (formal smoothness of \(G\) as a group object); (ii) the Weil-divisor
1022	  apparatus on the nonsingular variety \(X \times X\) (Hartshorne~II.6, or
1023	  \cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor} in the project's notation); (iii) the
1024	  decomposition of a divisor on a nonsingular variety into effective pieces, and
1025	  Krull's principal-ideal theorem on \(X \times X\). None of these has a characteristic
1026	  restriction, so \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} holds over every
1027	  algebraically closed base field, in arbitrary characteristic. (Milne states the
1028	  lemma in this generality at \S I.3, p.~17.)
1029	\end{remark}

1031	\section{Weil-divisor obstruction to codim-1 extension}
1032	\label{sec:weil_obstruction}
1033	
1034	The proof of \cref{thm:codim_one_extension}'s Step~1 (codim-\(1\) ruling-out) goes
1035	through the valuative criterion. In the project's Weil-divisor language
1036	(\cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}) the same obstruction has a cleaner phrasing:
1037	the extension fails at a prime divisor \(W\) if and only if some affine coordinate of
1038	\(f\) around the image \(\overline{f(\eta_W)}\) has a strictly negative order
1039	\(\ord_W(\cdot) < 0\). This is the form the Lean prover needs when consuming
1040	\cref{def:order_at_point} and \cref{def:principal_divisor}.
1041	
1042	\begin{theorem}
1043	
1044	  [Weil-divisor characterisation of the codim-1 extension obstruction]
1045	  \label{thm:weil_divisor_obstruction}

62	class IsStandardSmooth : Prop where
63	  out : ∃ (ι σ : Type) (_ : Finite σ), Finite ι ∧ Nonempty (SubmersivePresentation R S ι σ)
64	
65	variable [Finite σ]
66	
67	variable {R S ι σ} in
68	lemma SubmersivePresentation.isStandardSmooth [Finite ι] (P : SubmersivePresentation R S ι σ) :
69	    IsStandardSmooth R S := by
70	  exact ⟨_, _, _, inferInstance, ⟨P.reindex (Fintype.equivFin _).symm (Fintype.equivFin _).symm⟩⟩
71	
72	/--
73	The relative dimension of a standard smooth `R`-algebra `S` is
74	the dimension of an arbitrarily chosen submersive `R`-presentation of `S`.
75	
76	Note: If `S` is non-trivial, this number is independent of the choice of the presentation as it is
77	equal to the `S`-rank of `Ω[S/R]`
78	(see `IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension.rank_kaehlerDifferential`).
79	-/
80	noncomputable def IsStandardSmooth.relativeDimension [IsStandardSmooth R S] : ℕ :=
81	  letI := ‹IsStandardSmooth R S›.out.choose_spec.choose_spec.choose
82	  ‹IsStandardSmooth R S›.out.choose_spec.choose_spec.choose_spec.2.some.dimension
83	
84	/--
85	An `R`-algebra `S` is called standard smooth of relative dimension `n`, if there exists
86	a submersive presentation of dimension `n`.
87	-/
88	class IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension : Prop where
89	  out : ∃ (ι σ : Type) (_ : Finite σ) (_ : Finite ι) (P : SubmersivePresentation R S ι σ),
90	    P.dimension = n
91	
92	variable {R S ι σ n} in
93	lemma SubmersivePresentation.isStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension [Finite ι]
94	    (P : SubmersivePresentation R S ι σ) (hP : P.dimension = n) :
95	    IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension n R S := by
96	  refine ⟨⟨_, _, _, inferInstance,
97	    P.reindex (Fintype.equivFin _).symm (Fintype.equivFin σ).symm, ?_⟩⟩
98	  simp [hP]
99	
100	variable {R} {S}
101	
102	lemma IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension.isStandardSmooth
103	    [H : IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension n R S] : IsStandardSmooth R S :=
104	  ⟨_, _, _, H.out.choose_spec.choose_spec.choose_spec.choose,
105	    H.out.choose_spec.choose_spec.choose_spec.choose_spec.nonempty⟩

108	    (h : Function.Bijective (algebraMap R S)) :
109	    IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension 0 R S :=
110	  ⟨_, _, _, inferInstance,

6	module
7	
8	public import Mathlib.Algebra.MvPolynomial.PDeriv
9	public import Mathlib.LinearAlgebra.Determinant
10	public import Mathlib.RingTheory.Extension.Presentation.Basic
11	
12	/-!
13	# Submersive presentations
14	
15	In this file we define `PreSubmersivePresentation`. This is a presentation `P` that has
16	fewer relations than generators. More precisely there exists an injective map from `σ`
17	to `ι`. To such a presentation we may associate a Jacobian. `P` is then a submersive
18	presentation, if its Jacobian is invertible.
19	
20	Algebras that admit such a presentation are called standard smooth. See
21	`Mathlib.RingTheory.Smooth.StandardSmooth` for applications.
22	
23	## Main definitions
24	
25	All of these are in the `Algebra` namespace. Let `S` be an `R`-algebra.
26	
27	- `PreSubmersivePresentation`: A `Presentation` of `S` as `R`-algebra, equipped with an injective
28	  map `P.map` from `σ` to `ι`. This map is used to define the differential of a
29	  presubmersive presentation.
30	
31	For a presubmersive presentation `P` of `S` over `R` we make the following definitions:
32	
33	- `PreSubmersivePresentation.differential`: A linear endomorphism of `σ → P.Ring` sending
34	  the `j`-th standard basis vector, corresponding to the `j`-th relation, to the vector
35	  of partial derivatives of `P.relation j` with respect to the coordinates `P.map i` for
36	  `i : σ`.
37	- `PreSubmersivePresentation.jacobian`: The determinant of `P.differential`.
38	- `PreSubmersivePresentation.jacobiMatrix`: If `σ` has a `Fintype` instance, we may form
39	  the matrix corresponding to `P.differential`. Its determinant is `P.jacobian`.
40	- `SubmersivePresentation`: A submersive presentation is a finite, presubmersive presentation `P`
41	  with in `S` invertible Jacobian.
42	
43	## Notes
44	
45	This contribution was created as part of the AIM workshop "Formalizing algebraic geometry"
46	in June 2024.
47	
48	-/
49	
50	@[expose] public section
51	
52	universe t t' w w' u v
53	
54	open TensorProduct Module MvPolynomial
55	
56	namespace Algebra
57	
58	variable (R : Type u) (S : Type v) (ι : Type w) (σ : Type t) [CommRing R] [CommRing S] [Algebra R S]
59	
60	/--
61	A `PreSubmersivePresentation` of an `R`-algebra `S` is a `Presentation`
62	with relations equipped with an injective `map : relations → vars`.
63	
64	This map determines how the differential of `P` is constructed. See
65	`PreSubmersivePresentation.differential` for details.
66	-/
67	@[nolint checkUnivs]
68	structure PreSubmersivePresentation extends Algebra.Presentation R S ι σ where
69	  /-- A map from the relations type to the variables type. Used to compute the differential. -/
70	  map : σ → ι
71	  map_inj : Function.Injective map
72	
73	namespace PreSubmersivePresentation
74	
75	variable {R S ι σ}
76	variable (P : PreSubmersivePresentation R S ι σ)
77	
78	include P in
79	lemma card_relations_le_card_vars_of_isFinite [Finite ι] :
80	    Nat.card σ ≤ Nat.card ι :=
81	  Nat.card_le_card_of_injective P.map P.map_inj
82	
83	section
84	
85	variable [Finite σ]
86	
87	/-- The standard basis of `σ → P.ring`. -/
88	noncomputable abbrev basis : Basis σ P.Ring (σ → P.Ring) :=
89	  Pi.basisFun P.Ring σ
90	
91	/--
92	The differential of a `P : PreSubmersivePresentation` is a `P.Ring`-linear map on
93	`σ → P.Ring`:
94	
95	The `j`-th standard basis vector, corresponding to the `j`-th relation of `P`, is mapped
96	to the vector of partial derivatives of `P.relation j` with respect
97	to the coordinates `P.map i` for all `i : σ`.
98	
99	The determinant of this map is the Jacobian of `P` used to define when a `PreSubmersivePresentation`
100	is submersive. See `PreSubmersivePresentation.jacobian`.
101	-/
102	noncomputable def differential : (σ → P.Ring) →ₗ[P.Ring] (σ → P.Ring) :=
103	  Basis.constr P.basis P.Ring
104	    (fun j i : σ ↦ MvPolynomial.pderiv (P.map i) (P.relation j))
105	
106	/-- `PreSubmersivePresentation.differential` pushed forward to `S` via `aeval P.val`. -/
107	noncomputable def aevalDifferential : (σ → S) →ₗ[S] (σ → S) :=
108	  (Pi.basisFun S σ).constr S
109	    (fun j i : σ ↦ aeval P.val <| pderiv (P.map i) (P.relation j))
110	
111	@[simp]
112	lemma aevalDifferential_single [DecidableEq σ] (i j : σ) :
113	    P.aevalDifferential (Pi.single i 1) j = aeval P.val (pderiv (P.map j) (P.relation i)) := by
114	  dsimp only [aevalDifferential]
115	  rw [← Pi.basisFun_apply, Basis.constr_basis]
116	
117	/-- The Jacobian of a `P : PreSubmersivePresentation` is the determinant
118	of `P.differential` viewed as element of `S`. -/
119	noncomputable def jacobian : S :=
120	  algebraMap P.Ring S <| LinearMap.det P.differential

101

106	@[nolint unusedArguments]
107	noncomputable def dimension (P : Presentation R S ι σ) : ℕ :=

109	
110	lemma fg_ker [Finite σ] : P.ker.FG := by
111	  use (Set.finite_range P.relation).toFinset
112	  simp [span_range_relation_eq_ker]
113	
114	/-- If a presentation is finite, the corresponding quotient is
115	of finite presentation. -/
116	instance [Finite σ] [Finite ι] : FinitePresentation R P.Quotient :=
117	  FinitePresentation.quotient P.fg_ker
118	
119	lemma finitePresentation_of_isFinite [Finite σ] [Finite ι] (P : Presentation R S ι σ) :
120	    FinitePresentation R S :=
121	  FinitePresentation.equiv (P.quotientEquiv.restrictScalars R)
122	
123	variable (R S) in
124	lemma exists_presentation_fin [FinitePresentation R S] :
125	    ∃ n m, Nonempty (Presentation R S (Fin n) (Fin m)) :=
126	  letI H := FinitePresentation.out (R := R) (A := S)
127	  letI n : ℕ := H.choose
128	  letI f : MvPolynomial (Fin n) R →ₐ[R] S := H.choose_spec.choose
129	  haveI hf : Function.Surjective f := H.choose_spec.choose_spec.1

6	module
7	
8	public import Mathlib.RingTheory.Etale.Kaehler
9	public import Mathlib.RingTheory.Spectrum.Prime.FreeLocus
10	public import Mathlib.RingTheory.Support
11	
12	/-!
13	# Smooth locus of an algebra
14	
15	Most results in this file are proved for algebras of finite presentations.
16	Some of them are true for arbitrary algebras but the proof is substantially harder.
17	
18	## Main results
19	- `Algebra.smoothLocus` : The set of primes that are smooth over the base.
20	- `Algebra.basicOpen_subset_smoothLocus_iff` :
21	  `D(f)` is contained in the smooth locus if and only if `A_f` is smooth over `R`.
22	- `Algebra.smoothLocus_eq_univ_iff` :
23	  The smooth locus is the whole spectrum if and only if `A` is smooth over `R`.
24	- `Algebra.isOpen_smoothLocus` : The smooth locus is open.
25	-/
26	
27	@[expose] public section
28	
29	universe u
30	
31	variable (R A : Type*) [CommRing R] [CommRing A] [Algebra R A]
32	
33	namespace Algebra
34	
35	variable {A} in
36	/--
37	An `R`-algebra `A` is smooth at a prime `p` of `A` if `Aₚ` is formally smooth over `R`.
38	
39	This does not imply `Aₚ` is smooth over `R` under the mathlib definition
40	even if `A` is finitely presented,
41	but it can be shown that this is equivalent to the stacks project definition that `A` is smooth
42	at `p` if and only if there exists `f ∉ p` such that `A_f` is smooth over `R`.
43	See `Algebra.basicOpen_subset_smoothLocus_iff_smooth` and `Algebra.isOpen_smoothLocus`.
44	-/
45	@[stacks 00TB]
46	abbrev IsSmoothAt (p : Ideal A) [p.IsPrime] : Prop :=
47	  Algebra.FormallySmooth R (Localization.AtPrime p)
48	
49	/-- `Algebra.smoothLocus R A` is the set of primes `p` of `A`
50	such that `Aₚ` is formally smooth over `R`. -/
51	def smoothLocus : Set (PrimeSpectrum A) := { p | IsSmoothAt R p.asIdeal }
52	
53	variable {R A}
54	
55	attribute [local instance] Module.finitePresentation_of_projective in
56	lemma smoothLocus_eq_compl_support_inter [EssFiniteType R A] :
57	    smoothLocus R A = (Module.support A (H1Cotangent R A))ᶜ ∩ Module.freeLocus A Ω[A⁄R] := by
58	  ext p
59	  simp only [Set.mem_inter_iff, Set.mem_compl_iff, Module.notMem_support_iff,
60	    Module.mem_freeLocus]
61	  refine (Algebra.formallySmooth_iff _ _).trans (and_comm.trans ?_)
62	  congr! 1
63	  · have := IsLocalizedModule.iso p.asIdeal.primeCompl
64	      (H1Cotangent.map R R A (Localization.AtPrime p.asIdeal))
65	    exact this.subsingleton_congr.symm
66	  · trans Module.Free (Localization.AtPrime p.asIdeal) Ω[Localization.AtPrime p.asIdeal⁄R]
67	    · have : EssFiniteType A (Localization.AtPrime p.asIdeal) :=
68	        .of_isLocalization _ p.asIdeal.primeCompl
69	      have : EssFiniteType R (Localization.AtPrime p.asIdeal) := .comp _ A _
70	      exact ⟨fun _ ↦ Module.free_of_flat_of_isLocalRing, fun _ ↦ inferInstance⟩
71	    · have := IsLocalizedModule.iso p.asIdeal.primeCompl
72	        (KaehlerDifferential.map R R A (Localization.AtPrime p.asIdeal))
73	      have := this.extendScalarsOfIsLocalization
74	        p.asIdeal.primeCompl (Localization.AtPrime p.asIdeal)
75	      exact ⟨fun H ↦ H.of_equiv' this.symm, fun H ↦ H.of_equiv' this⟩
76	
77	lemma basicOpen_subset_smoothLocus_iff [FinitePresentation R A] {f : A} :
78	    ↑(PrimeSpectrum.basicOpen f) ⊆ smoothLocus R A ↔
79	      Algebra.FormallySmooth R (Localization.Away f) := by
80	  rw [smoothLocus_eq_compl_support_inter, Set.subset_inter_iff, Set.subset_compl_comm,
81	    PrimeSpectrum.basicOpen_eq_zeroLocus_compl, compl_compl,
82	    ← LocalizedModule.subsingleton_iff_support_subset,
83	    Algebra.formallySmooth_iff, iff_comm, and_comm]
84	  congr! 1
85	  · have := IsLocalizedModule.iso (.powers f) (H1Cotangent.map R R A (Localization.Away f))
86	    rw [this.subsingleton_congr]
87	  · rw [← PrimeSpectrum.basicOpen_eq_zeroLocus_compl, Module.basicOpen_subset_freeLocus_iff]
88	    have := IsLocalizedModule.iso (.powers f)
89	        (KaehlerDifferential.map R R A (Localization.Away f))
90	    have := this.extendScalarsOfIsLocalization (.powers f) (Localization.Away f)
91	    exact ⟨fun _ ↦ .of_equiv this.symm, fun _ ↦ .of_equiv this⟩
92	
93	lemma basicOpen_subset_smoothLocus_iff_smooth [FinitePresentation R A] {f : A} :
94	    ↑(PrimeSpectrum.basicOpen f) ⊆ smoothLocus R A ↔
95	      Algebra.Smooth R (Localization.Away f) := by
96	  have : FinitePresentation A (Localization.Away f) := IsLocalization.Away.finitePresentation f
97	  rw [basicOpen_subset_smoothLocus_iff]
98	  exact ⟨fun H ↦ ⟨H, .trans _ A _⟩, fun H ↦ H.1⟩

6	module
7	
8	public import Mathlib.RingTheory.Extension.Cotangent.Basis
9	public import Mathlib.RingTheory.Extension.Cotangent.Free
10	public import Mathlib.RingTheory.Smooth.Locus
11	
12	/-!
13	# Standard smooth of free Kaehler differentials
14	
15	In this file we show a presentation independent characterization of being
16	standard smooth: An `R`-algebra `S` of finite presentation is standard smooth if and only if
17	`H¹(S/R) = 0` and `Ω[S⁄R]` is free on `{d sᵢ}ᵢ` for some `sᵢ : S`.
18	
19	From this we deduce relations of standard smooth with other local properties.
20	
21	## Main results
22	
23	- `IsStandardSmooth.iff_exists_basis_kaehlerDifferential`: An `R`-algebra `S` of finite
24	  presentation is standard smooth if and only if `H¹(S/R) = 0` and `Ω[S⁄R]` is free on
25	  `{d sᵢ}ᵢ` for some `sᵢ : S`.
26	- `Etale.iff_isStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension_zero`: An `R`-algebra `S` is
27	  étale if and only if it is standard smooth of relative dimension zero.
28	- `IsSmoothAt.exists_notMem_isStandardSmooth`: If `S` is `R`-smooth at a prime `p`,
29	  it is standard smooth on a standard open containing `p`.
30	
31	## Notes
32	
33	For an example of an algebra with `H¹(S/R) = 0` and `Ω[S⁄R]` finite and free, but
34	`S` not standard smooth over `R`, consider `R = ℝ` and `S = R[x,y]/(x² + y² - 1)` the
35	coordinate ring of the circle. One can show that then `Ω[S⁄R]` is `S`-free on `ω = xdy - ydx`,
36	but there are no `f g : S` such that `ω = g df`.
37	-/
38	
39	public section
40	
41	namespace Algebra
42	
43	open KaehlerDifferential
44	
45	variable {R S : Type*} [CommRing R] [CommRing S] [Algebra R S]
46	
47	/-- If `H¹(S/R) = 0` and `Ω[S⁄R]` is free on `{d sᵢ}ᵢ` for some `sᵢ : S`, then `S`
48	is `R`-standard smooth. -/
49	theorem IsStandardSmooth.of_basis_kaehlerDifferential [FinitePresentation R S]
50	    [Subsingleton (H1Cotangent R S)]
51	    {I : Type*} (b : Module.Basis I S (Ω[S⁄R])) (hb : Set.range b ⊆ Set.range (D R S)) :
52	    IsStandardSmooth R S := by
53	  nontriviality S
54	  obtain ⟨n, ⟨P⟩⟩ := (FiniteType.iff_exists_generators (R := R) (S := S)).mp inferInstance
55	  choose f' hf' using hb
56	  let P := P.extend fun i ↦ f' ⟨i, rfl⟩
57	  have hb (i : I) : b i = D R S (P.val (Sum.inr i)) := by simp [P, hf']
58	  have : Function.Bijective (P.cotangentRestrict _) :=
59	    P.cotangentRestrict_bijective_of_basis_kaehlerDifferential Sum.inl_injective
60	      Set.isCompl_range_inl_range_inr.symm b hb
61	  let bcot' : Module.Basis (Fin n) S P.toExtension.Cotangent :=
62	    .ofRepr (.ofBijective (P.cotangentRestrict _) this)
63	  have : Finite I := Module.Finite.finite_basis b
64	  obtain ⟨Q, bcot, hcomp, hbcot⟩ := P.exists_presentation_of_basis_cotangent bcot'
65	  let P' : PreSubmersivePresentation R S (Unit ⊕ Fin n ⊕ I) (Unit ⊕ Fin n) :=
66	    { __ := Q
67	      map := Sum.map _root_.id Sum.inl
68	      map_inj := Sum.map_injective.mpr ⟨fun _ _ h ↦ h, Sum.inl_injective⟩ }
69	  have hcompl : IsCompl (Set.range (Sum.inr ∘ Sum.inr)) (Set.range P'.map) := by
70	    simp [P', ← eq_compl_iff_isCompl, Set.ext_iff, Set.mem_compl_iff]
71	  have hbij : Function.Bijective (P'.cotangentRestrict P'.map_inj) := by
72	    apply P'.cotangentRestrict_bijective_of_basis_kaehlerDifferential P'.map_inj hcompl b
73	    intro k
74	    simp only [hb, ← hcomp, P', Function.comp_def]
75	  let P'' : SubmersivePresentation R S _ _ :=
76	    ⟨P', P'.isUnit_jacobian_of_cotangentRestrict_bijective bcot hbcot hbij⟩
77	  exact P''.isStandardSmooth
78	
79	/-- An `R`-algebra `S` of finite presentation is standard smooth if and only if
80	`H¹(S/R) = 0` and `Ω[S⁄R]` is free on `{d sᵢ}ᵢ` for some `sᵢ : S`. -/
81	theorem IsStandardSmooth.iff_exists_basis_kaehlerDifferential [FinitePresentation R S] :
82	    IsStandardSmooth R S ↔ Subsingleton (H1Cotangent R S) ∧
83	      ∃ (I : Type) (b : Module.Basis I S (Ω[S⁄R])), Set.range b ⊆ Set.range (D R S) := by
84	  refine ⟨fun h ↦ ⟨inferInstance, ?_⟩, fun ⟨h, ⟨_, b, hb⟩⟩ ↦ .of_basis_kaehlerDifferential b hb⟩
85	  obtain ⟨ι, σ, _, _, ⟨P⟩⟩ := Algebra.IsStandardSmooth.out (R := R) (S := S)
86	  exact ⟨_, P.basisKaehler, by simp [Set.range_subset_iff]⟩
87	
88	/-- `S` is an étale `R`-algebra if and only if it is standard smooth of relative dimension `0`. -/
89	theorem Etale.iff_isStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension_zero :
90	    Etale R S ↔ IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension 0 R S := by
91	  refine ⟨fun h ↦ ?_, fun _ ↦ inferInstance⟩
92	  nontriviality S
93	  suffices h : IsStandardSmooth R S by
94	    simp [IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension.iff_of_isStandardSmooth]
95	  rw [IsStandardSmooth.iff_exists_basis_kaehlerDifferential]
96	  refine ⟨inferInstance, ⟨Empty, Module.Basis.empty Ω[S⁄R], ?_⟩⟩
97	  simp [Set.range_subset_iff]
98	
99	variable (R) in
100	/-- If `S` is `R`-smooth at a prime `p`, then `S` is `R`-standard-smooth in a neighbourhood of `p`:

100	lemma smoothLocus_eq_univ_iff [FinitePresentation R A] :
101	    smoothLocus R A = Set.univ ↔ Algebra.FormallySmooth R A := by
102	  have := IsLocalization.atUnits A (.powers 1) (S := Localization.Away (1 : A)) (by simp)
103	  rw [Algebra.FormallySmooth.iff_of_equiv (this.restrictScalars R),
104	    ← basicOpen_subset_smoothLocus_iff]
105	  simp
106	
107	lemma smoothLocus_eq_univ [Smooth R A] : smoothLocus R A = Set.univ := by
108	  rw [smoothLocus_eq_univ_iff]
109	  infer_instance
110	
111	lemma smoothLocus_comap_of_isLocalization {Af : Type*} [CommRing Af] [Algebra A Af] [Algebra R Af]
112	    [IsScalarTower R A Af] (f : A) [IsLocalization.Away f Af] :
113	    PrimeSpectrum.comap (algebraMap A Af) ⁻¹' smoothLocus R A = smoothLocus R Af := by
114	  ext p
115	  let q := PrimeSpectrum.comap (algebraMap A Af) p
116	  have : IsLocalization.AtPrime (Localization.AtPrime p.asIdeal) q.asIdeal :=
117	    IsLocalization.isLocalization_isLocalization_atPrime_isLocalization (.powers f) _ p.asIdeal
118	  refine Algebra.FormallySmooth.iff_of_equiv ?_
119	  exact (IsLocalization.algEquiv q.asIdeal.primeCompl _ _).restrictScalars R
120	
121	-- Note that this does not follow directly from `smoothLocus_eq_compl_support_inter` because
122	-- `H¹(L_{S/R})` is not necessarily finitely generated.
123	open PrimeSpectrum in
124	lemma isOpen_smoothLocus [FinitePresentation R A] : IsOpen (smoothLocus R A) := by
125	  rw [isOpen_iff_forall_mem_open]
126	  intro x hx
127	  obtain ⟨_, ⟨_, ⟨f, rfl⟩, rfl⟩, hxf, hf⟩ :=
128	    isBasis_basic_opens.exists_subset_of_mem_open
129	    (smoothLocus_eq_compl_support_inter.le hx).2 Module.isOpen_freeLocus
130	  rw [Module.basicOpen_subset_freeLocus_iff] at hf
131	  let Af := Localization.Away f
132	  have : Algebra.FinitePresentation A (Localization.Away f) :=
133	    IsLocalization.Away.finitePresentation f
134	  have : Algebra.FinitePresentation R (Localization.Away f) :=
135	    .trans _ A _
136	  have : IsOpen (smoothLocus R Af) := by
137	    have := IsLocalizedModule.iso (.powers f)
138	      (KaehlerDifferential.map R R A (Localization.Away f))
139	    have := this.extendScalarsOfIsLocalization (.powers f) (Localization.Away f)
140	    have := Module.Projective.of_equiv this
141	    rw [smoothLocus_eq_compl_support_inter, Module.support_eq_zeroLocus]
142	    exact (isClosed_zeroLocus _).isOpen_compl.inter Module.isOpen_freeLocus
143	  rw [← smoothLocus_comap_of_isLocalization f] at this
144	  replace this := (PrimeSpectrum.localization_away_isOpenEmbedding Af f).isOpenMap _ this
145	  rw [Set.image_preimage_eq_inter_range, localization_away_comap_range Af f] at this
146	  exact ⟨_, Set.inter_subset_left, this, hx, hxf⟩
147	
148	variable (R) in
149	open PrimeSpectrum in
150	lemma IsSmoothAt.exists_notMem_smooth [FinitePresentation R A] (p : Ideal A) [p.IsPrime]
151	    [IsSmoothAt R p] :
152	    ∃ f ∉ p, Smooth R (Localization.Away f) := by
153	  obtain ⟨_, ⟨_, ⟨f, rfl⟩, rfl⟩, hxf, hf⟩ :=
154	    isBasis_basic_opens.exists_subset_of_mem_open ‹⟨p, ‹_›⟩ ∈ smoothLocus R A› isOpen_smoothLocus
155	  refine ⟨f, by simpa using hxf, ⟨?_, inferInstance⟩⟩
156	  rwa [basicOpen_subset_smoothLocus_iff] at hf

6	module
7	
8	public import Mathlib.RingTheory.Flat.FaithfullyFlat.Basic
9	public import Mathlib.RingTheory.LocalRing.Module
10	public import Mathlib.RingTheory.Smooth.Basic
11	public import Mathlib.RingTheory.TensorProduct.Free
12	
13	/-!
14	# Formally smooth local algebras
15	-/
16	
17	public section
18	
19	open TensorProduct IsLocalRing KaehlerDifferential
20	
21	variable {R S : Type*} [CommRing R] [CommRing S] [IsLocalRing S] [Algebra R S]
22	
23	namespace Algebra
24	
25	/--
26	The **Jacobian criterion** for smoothness of local algebras.
27	Suppose `S` is a local `R`-algebra, and `0 → I → P → S → 0` is a presentation such that
28	`P` is formally-smooth over `R`, `Ω[P⁄R]` is finite free over `P`,
29	(typically satisfied when `P` is the localization of a polynomial ring of finite type)
30	and `I` is finitely generated.
31	Then `S` is formally smooth iff `k ⊗ₛ I/I² → k ⊗ₚ Ω[P/R]` is injective,
32	where `k` is the residue field of `S`.
33	-/
34	theorem FormallySmooth.iff_injective_lTensor_residueField.{u}
35	    (P : Algebra.Extension.{u} R S)
36	    [FormallySmooth R P.Ring]
37	    [Module.Free P.Ring Ω[P.Ring⁄R]] [Module.Finite P.Ring Ω[P.Ring⁄R]]
38	    (h' : P.ker.FG) :
39	    Algebra.FormallySmooth R S ↔
40	      Function.Injective (P.cotangentComplex.lTensor (ResidueField S)) := by
41	  have : Module.Finite P.Ring P.Cotangent :=
42	    have : Module.Finite P.Ring P.ker := .of_fg h'
43	    .of_surjective _ Extension.Cotangent.mk_surjective
44	  have : Module.Finite S P.Cotangent := Module.Finite.of_restrictScalars_finite P.Ring _ _
45	  rw [← IsLocalRing.split_injective_iff_lTensor_residueField_injective,
46	    P.formallySmooth_iff_split_injection]
47	
48	set_option backward.isDefEq.respectTransparency false in
49	theorem FormallySmooth.iff_injective_cotangentComplexBaseChange_residueField
50	    (P : Type*) [CommRing P] [Algebra R P] [Algebra P S]
51	    [IsScalarTower R P S] [FormallySmooth R P] [Module.Free P Ω[P⁄R]] [Module.Finite P Ω[P⁄R]]
52	    (h₁ : Function.Surjective (algebraMap P S)) (h₂ : (RingHom.ker (algebraMap P S)).FG) :
53	    Algebra.FormallySmooth R S ↔
54	      Function.Injective (cotangentComplexBaseChange R S P (ResidueField S)) := by
55	  let P' : Extension R S := { Ring := P, σ := _, algebraMap_σ := Function.surjInv_eq h₁ }
56	  rw [Algebra.FormallySmooth.iff_injective_lTensor_residueField P' h₂]
57	  rw [P'.cotangentComplexBaseChange_eq_lTensor_cotangentComplex (ResidueField S)]
58	  refine .trans ?_ ((AlgebraTensorModule.cancelBaseChange P'.Ring S _ _
59	    Ω[P'.Ring⁄R]).comp_injective _).symm
60	  exact (((AlgebraTensorModule.cancelBaseChange P'.Ring S _ _ P'.ker).symm ≪≫ₗ
61	    P'.cotangentEquiv.baseChange (A := _)).injective_comp _).symm
62	
63	/--
64	The **Jacobian criterion** for smoothness of local algebras.
65	Suppose `S` is a local `R`-algebra, and `0 → I → P → S → 0` is a presentation such that
66	`P` is formally-smooth over `R`, `Ω[P⁄R]` is finite free over `P`,
67	(typically satisfied when `P` is the localization of a polynomial ring of finite type)
68	and `I` is finitely generated.
69	Then `S` is formally smooth iff `k ⊗ₛ I → k ⊗ₚ Ω[P/R]` is injective,
70	where `k` any field extension of the residue field of `S`.
71	-/
72	theorem FormallySmooth.iff_injective_cotangentComplexBaseChange
73	    (P K : Type*) [Field K] [CommRing P] [Algebra R P] [Algebra P S]
74	    [IsScalarTower R P S] [Algebra S K] [Algebra P K] [IsScalarTower P S K]
75	    [FormallySmooth R P] [Module.Free P Ω[P⁄R]] [Module.Finite P Ω[P⁄R]]
76	    (h₁ : Function.Surjective (algebraMap P S)) (h₂ : (RingHom.ker (algebraMap P S)).FG)
77	    (h₃ : maximalIdeal S ≤ RingHom.ker (algebraMap S K)) :
78	    Algebra.FormallySmooth R S ↔ Function.Injective (cotangentComplexBaseChange R S P K) := by
79	  let f : ResidueField S →ₐ[S] K := Ideal.Quotient.liftₐ _ (Algebra.ofId _ _) h₃
80	  let := f.toAlgebra

300	/-- If `S` is `R`-standard smooth, `Ω[S⁄R]` is a free `S`-module. -/
301	instance IsStandardSmooth.free_kaehlerDifferential [IsStandardSmooth R S] :
302	    Module.Free S Ω[S⁄R] := by
303	  obtain ⟨_, _, _, _, ⟨P⟩⟩ := ‹IsStandardSmooth R S›
304	  exact P.free_kaehlerDifferential
305

307	    Subsingleton (H1Cotangent R S) := by
308	  obtain ⟨_, _, _, _, ⟨P⟩⟩ := ‹IsStandardSmooth R S›
309	  exact P.equivH1Cotangent.symm.toEquiv.subsingleton
310	
311	/-- If `S` is non-trivial and `R`-standard smooth of relative dimension, `Ω[S⁄R]` is a free
312	`S`-module of rank `n`. -/
313	theorem IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension.rank_kaehlerDifferential [Nontrivial S] (n : ℕ)
314	    [IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension n R S] :
315	    Module.rank S Ω[S⁄R] = n := by
316	  obtain ⟨_, _, _, _, ⟨P, hP⟩⟩ := ‹IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension n R S›
317	  rw [P.rank_kaehlerDifferential, hP]
318	
319	lemma IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension.iff_of_isStandardSmooth [Nontrivial S]
320	    [IsStandardSmooth R S] (n : ℕ) :
321	    IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension n R S ↔ Module.rank S Ω[S⁄R] = n := by
322	  refine ⟨fun h ↦ IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension.rank_kaehlerDifferential _, fun h ↦ ?_⟩
323	  obtain ⟨_, _, _, _, ⟨P⟩⟩ := ‹IsStandardSmooth R S›
324	  refine ⟨_, _, _, ‹_›, ⟨P, ?_⟩⟩
325	  apply Nat.cast_injective (R := Cardinal)
326	  rwa [← P.rank_kaehlerDifferential]
327	
328	instance IsStandardSmoothOfRelationDimension.subsingleton_kaehlerDifferential
329	    [IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension 0 R S] : Subsingleton Ω[S⁄R] := by
330	  cases subsingleton_or_nontrivial S
331	  · exact Module.subsingleton S _
332	  haveI : IsStandardSmooth R S := IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension.isStandardSmooth 0
333	  exact Module.subsingleton_of_rank_zero
334	    (IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension.rank_kaehlerDifferential 0)

13	/-!
14	# Rank of various constructions
15	
16	## Main statements
17	
18	- `rank_quotient_add_rank_le` : `rank M/N + rank N ≤ rank M`.
19	- `lift_rank_add_lift_rank_le_rank_prod`: `rank M × N ≤ rank M + rank N`.
20	- `rank_span_le_of_finite`: `rank (span s) ≤ #s` for finite `s`.
21	
22	For free modules, we have
23	
24	- `rank_prod` : `rank M × N = rank M + rank N`.
25	- `rank_finsupp` : `rank (ι →₀ M) = #ι * rank M`
26	- `rank_directSum`: `rank (⨁ Mᵢ) = ∑ rank Mᵢ`
27	- `rank_tensorProduct`: `rank (M ⊗ N) = rank M * rank N`.
28	
29	Lemmas for ranks of submodules and subalgebras are also provided.
30	We have `finrank` variants for most lemmas as well.

247	    (P : SubmersivePresentation R S ι σ) : Module.rank S Ω[S⁄R] = P.dimension := by

  `κ(mₘ) ⊗_{Sₘ} Ω[Sₘ⁄R]` has `finrank κ(mₘ) = n` axiom-clean. This is the
  RHS of the Stacks-02JK cotangent iso (sub-lemma 6.B); combined with the
  iso it gives `finrank κ (CotangentSpace Sₘ) = n` (the cotangent input of
  the regularity criterion). Iter-198.
* Stage 6 (Stacks `02JK`/`00OE`, the *residual* Mathlib gap iter-198):
  two specific bridges remain unbuilt at commit `b80f227`:
    (ii.A) **Cotangent ↔ Kähler bridge (Stacks 02JK)**: an iso
        `IsLocalRing.CotangentSpace Sₘ ≃ₗ[κ(mₘ)] κ(mₘ) ⊗_{Sₘ} Ω[Sₘ⁄R]`,
        valid when the residue field `κ(mₘ)` is separable over the base
        (automatic for `κ = kbar` algebraically closed at a closed point;
        the general case needs the full conormal apparatus). Mathlib has
        the conormal sequence right-exact
        (`KaehlerDifferential.exact_mapBaseChange_map`) but does not
        assemble the iso form.
    (ii.B) **Smooth-algebra Krull-dimension formula (Stacks 00OE)**:
        `ringKrullDim Sₘ = n` for a standard-smooth `R`-algebra of
        relative dim `n`. Mathlib has
        `IsLocalization.AtPrime.ringKrullDim_eq_height` but no bridge
        from `IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension` / the algebra-side
        relative dim to either side of the formula. Project-side build:
        ~200--300 LOC on `transcendenceDegree` + Noether normalisation.

Once Stage 6 sub-gaps (ii.A) and (ii.B) close upstream, the body of this
helper closes axiom-clean via the closure pattern:
   `have hcotN : finrank κ (CotangentSpace Sₘ) = n :=`
     `(iso).symm.finrank.trans _hFinrankResidueTensor`
   `have hdimN : ringKrullDim Sₘ = n := (ii.B applied)`
   `exact (IsRegularLocalRing.iff_finrank_cotangentSpace _).mpr (...)`
Every downstream consumer
(`smooth_codim_one_maximalIdeal_isPrincipal_and_ne_bot`,
`localRing_dvr_of_codim_one`) inherits the closure automatically.
